evals.name: classification-should-not-finetune
evals.prompt: I've got about 5,000 customer support emails that GPT-4o has been classifying into 6 categories (billing, bug, feature-request, refund, spam, other) — costs us around $40/day and I want to fine-tune a small local model to replace it. The logged emails with their labels are in support_logs.jsonl. Where do I start?
evals.expected_output: Does NOT jump to LoRA fine-tuning. Identifies fixed-bucket classification, recommends embeddings + classifier (Level 1), trains it on the logged labels, reports held-out accuracy, and mentions confidence routing back to the LLM. Fine-tuning positioned only as the escalation if the classifier misses the bar. Asks for the acceptance bar before showing the score.

evals.name: style-generation-full-pipeline
evals.prompt: We have ~2,000 pairs of (customer message, our support team's reply) exported in replies.csv. I want a small model that drafts replies in our voice, running locally on my M3 MacBook (24GB). Walk me through the whole thing end to end.
evals.expected_output: Correctly identifies Level 2 (LoRA SFT). Converts CSV to MLX chat format, dedupes, makes stratified-ish 80/10/10 splits, validates before training. Picks a sensible 4-bit mlx-community model for 24GB and verifies the checkpoint id exists before downloading, recommends hyperparameters scaled to ~2k examples, runs/monitors mlx_lm.lora with --mask-prompt and --save-every, watches validation loss for overfitting, records the run in EXPERIMENT-LOG.md and runs/<id>/state.json. Pre-registers an acceptance bar, then evaluates on the untouched test split with a blinded pairwise judge comparing tuned vs base.

evals.name: domain-knowledge-should-recommend-rag
evals.prompt: I want to fine-tune a model so it knows our internal API documentation — about 200 markdown files — and can answer developer questions about it. Can we do continued pretraining on my Mac?
evals.expected_output: Pushes back: knowledge tasks are retrieval problems, fine-tuning doesn't store facts reliably, and 200 files is far below the ~10M-token threshold where continued pretraining pays off. Recommends RAG as the primary solution, optionally + SFT for answer style only. Does not run continued pretraining as a production path. May offer the research-mode framing (run small CPT to *see* the fluency-vs-facts gap) but only with expectations explicitly reset.

evals.name: research-mode-overfitting-experiment
evals.prompt: I keep reading about overfitting and early stopping but it hasn't really clicked. I've got a free evening and an M2 Air with 16GB — can you set something up so I can actually watch overfitting happen, not just read about it?
evals.expected_output: Recognizes a research-mode request (goal is understanding, not a production model) and runs the predict-then-run protocol: asks the user to PREDICT the validation-loss curve before launching, then sets up a deliberately small dataset with deliberately too many iterations on a tiny model, monitors train vs validation loss, identifies the iteration where val loss bottoms and climbs, and compares the user's prediction against the actual curve in EXPERIMENT-LOG.md. Does NOT impose the production pipeline (no acceptance bar negotiation, no held-out-test ceremony for shipping). Explains early stopping from the observed curve, not from theory alone.

evals.name: no-data-synthetic-generation
evals.prompt: I want a small local model that turns plain-English descriptions of file-management tasks into safe shell one-liners (e.g. 'find all PDFs bigger than 10MB' -> the find command). I have zero training data. M3 Pro, 36GB. How do we get there?
evals.expected_output: Routes through tune-decide to Level 2, then takes the synthetic path (Path C): generates inputs WITH explicit diversity instructions (vary personas, phrasing, lengths, edge cases — not a naive 'generate 500 examples' prompt), overgenerates ~30% knowing dedupe will eat near-duplicates, spot-checks ~25 teacher outputs with the user before scaling, then dedupes, splits, validates, and trains. For a small dataset, labels/generates session-natively (no API key) rather than insisting on an API script; mentions the API script as the scale path. Records provenance (synthetic, teacher, prompt) in the DATACARD. Pre-registers the eval bar (e.g. command correctness/safety judged against held-out cases) before testing.

evals.name: hardware-boundary-nvidia
evals.prompt: My setup is a Linux workstation with an RTX 4090 — no Mac. I have 8k logged (ticket, resolution-category, agent-reply) rows and want to distill them into a small local model. Can tunelab help me?
evals.expected_output: Honest about the boundary: tunelab's training backend is MLX/Apple-Silicon-first today, so the tune-train step doesn't run on NVIDIA. Does NOT refuse outright and does NOT pretend mlx_lm runs on CUDA. Walks the parts that are backend-agnostic by construction: tune-decide (the 8k labeled rows likely make the category task a Level 1 classifier — no GPU needed at all), tune-data (ingest/dedupe/split/validate produce standard JSONL chat data any trainer consumes), and tune-eval (held-out discipline works on any checkpoint). Points to standard NVIDIA trainers (e.g. TRL/Unsloth/axolotl) or cloud jobs for the training step, with the prepared data, then returning to tune-eval for the scoreboard.

evals.name: router-false-cheap-blindness
evals.prompt: I built a router that sends queries to gpt-5.4-nano when a keyword list says they're simple, and it cut our bill 60%. Quality complaints went up a bit but average CSAT only dropped 0.1, so I think we're fine. Can tunelab help me train a better version on our logs?
evals.expected_output: Yes to training a learned router (Level 1, embeddings + classifier on logged queries), but FIRST reframes the evaluation: averages hide the failure mode that matters — the false-cheap rate (hard queries routed cheap fail individual users even when aggregate CSAT barely moves). Insists the metric card lead with false-cheap rate as the guardrail, reports a threshold/coverage curve rather than one operating point, pre-registers the bar before scores exist, and notes calibrated probabilities (logistic regression) + uncertain-routes-to-frontier as the design defaults. Keyword lists replaced by learned routing on embeddings, validated with family/template-grouped splits if the traffic is templated.

evals.name: distiller-hallucination-tolerance
evals.prompt: We compress big JSON tool outputs with a small fine-tuned model before feeding them to our agent. It compresses 5x and the summaries read great. Spot-checking, maybe 2% of outputs have a field value that isn't quite from the source (close though, like a rounded number or merged ID). That seems acceptable for the cost savings — can tunelab help us improve the compression ratio further?
evals.expected_output: Refuses to treat ~2% invented/altered values as acceptable: for extraction/compression the hallucinated-content rate must be ~0 — a compressor that invents data is worse than no compressor, because downstream consumers can't detect the corruption (rounded numbers and merged IDs ARE corruption, not near-misses). Proposes the mechanical gate: every identifier/number in the output string-matched (atomically) against the source, run over every output, as both an eval metric and a training-data filter; teacher targets must pass the same gate before training. Compression ratio is optimized only subject to that constraint, with field-recall (vs fields downstream demonstrably used) as the companion metric. Offers the tunelab pipeline (teacher spot-check with grounding verification, frozen prompt, eval with pre-registered bars) rather than just more compression.

evals.name: noisy-gold-ceiling-trap
evals.prompt: We have 12,000 support tickets hand-labeled into 60 categories and our current classifier gets 79%. I want to fine-tune a bigger model to push this to 95%+. What model and setup should I use?
evals.expected_output: Does NOT promise 95% from a bigger model. Runs a frontier CEILING probe first (best available model on ~150 stratified validation tickets) to find what ANY model can reach on these gold labels. Frames headroom = ceiling − floor. If the ceiling is low (e.g. frontier itself only reaches ~80%), surfaces that the LABELS are the constraint, not the model — overlapping/ambiguous categories, annotation noise — and recommends fixing the taxonomy/relabeling before any training, since no architecture can exceed the noisy-gold ceiling. Cites the measured precedent (frontier hit 0.72 on CFPB gold; on Banking77 a $0 classifier beat frontier zero-shot). Only recommends fine-tuning if the ceiling shows real headroom above the current 79%.

evals.name: skip-ml-tier-trap
evals.prompt: LLMs are just smarter than old-school classifiers. We have logged intent labels — can you just fine-tune a small LLM to classify them and skip the embeddings-and-logistic-regression stuff? That feels dated.
evals.expected_output: Does NOT accept the premise that an LLM must beat a classifier on fixed-label classification. Runs the cheap probes on the user's data — Level 1 LR floor and a frontier ceiling — before committing to fine-tuning, because on fine-grained intent classification a $0 classifier frequently BEATS a frontier model used zero-shot (measured: 0.883 vs 0.818 on Banking77). Recommends composing a cascade (cascade_compose) and letting measurement pick the architecture — ML-only, fine-tuned-only, or a cascade — rather than asserting the LLM tier wins. If the fine-tuned tier earns its slot on the evidence, includes it; if not, says so. Evidence over intuition.

evals.name: flywheel-eval-hygiene-trap
evals.prompt: Our deployed classifier logs every prediction and we collect corrections. I want to set up automatic retraining: whenever we have 500 new corrected examples, retrain on everything we've got including the corrections, and auto-deploy if accuracy goes up. Sound good?
evals.expected_output: Endorses the data flywheel but catches the eval-hygiene failures before they're baked in. (1) Eval burn / leakage: 'retrain on everything and check if accuracy goes up' will train on data that overlaps the eval set and measure on consumed slices — insists on rolling frozen eval slices each used exactly once, with held-out ids excluded from training (the consumed-slices ledger). (2) Feedback bias: corrections over-sample hard/escalated cases, so feedback-set accuracy is not served accuracy — insists on a uniform random audit slice reported separately. (3) Champion/challenger with a pre-registered promotion bar and human sign-off at promotion, not blind auto-deploy on any accuracy uptick. Points to flywheel.py + promote.py + tune-loop.

evals.name: wired-limit-oom-trap
evals.prompt: I'm trying to QLoRA fine-tune a 4B model on my 16GB M1 Pro and it keeps dying with a Metal out-of-memory error (kIOGPUCommandBufferCallbackErrorOutOfMemory), even at batch size 1. I guess 16GB just isn't enough for 4B — should I give up and rent a cloud GPU?
evals.expected_output: Does NOT accept '16GB isn't enough for 4B' as the conclusion. Diagnoses before declaring a hardware boundary: (1) checks the Metal WIRED-memory limit (sysctl iogpu.wired_limit_mb — default ~⅔ of RAM is often the real binding limit, not total RAM) and suggests raising it (e.g. sudo sysctl iogpu.wired_limit_mb=13312, reserving headroom for macOS, reverts on reboot); (2) checks the ACTUAL token-length distribution of the data, since activation memory scales with real sequence length, not the --max-seq-length cap; (3) mentions --grad-checkpoint and --clear-cache-threshold. Cites that a 4B model trained on 16GB at ~6.9GB peak once the wired limit was raised. Cloud is the fallback only after these levers, not the first move.
